1: raqia asher al-rosh hakeruvim ('the firmament that was over the heads of the cherubim'): the same raqia (expanse/firmament) that appeared in 1:22 above the four living creatures. The identification of the creatures here as keruvim (cherubim) — which will be made explicit in v. 20 — bridges the throne vision of chapter 1 to the temple context of chapters 8-11. even sapir kemar'eh ('like sapphire stone in appearance'): the sapphire-blue throne platform connecting to the throne-chariot.
2: galgelei esh ('burning coals of fire'): the man in linen is commanded to take fire from between the cherubim and scatter it over the city — the same coals that will be scattered over Jerusalem as judgment. The fire comes from within the divine chariot, making the judgment itself an emanation of divine holiness. What was the fire of divine presence becomes the fire of divine judgment when directed toward an apostate city.
3: ha'anan male et-hechatzer hap'nimit ('the cloud filled the inner court'): the glory-cloud (anan) fills the inner court just as it filled the tabernacle at its dedication (Ex 40:34-35) and the temple at Solomon's dedication (1 Kgs 8:10-11). But here the filling of the cloud precedes the departure of the glory — it is a last manifestation of divine presence before withdrawal, not an inaugural occupation.
4: varam kevod YHWH me'al hakerub al-miftan habayit ('the glory of YHWH rose from upon the cherub to the threshold of the house'): the second stage of the glory's departure — from the cherub (the ark/mercy seat) to the threshold (the doorway of the temple). The glory is moving toward the exit. vatimale habayit et-he'anan ('the house was filled with the cloud'): paradoxically, as the glory prepares to leave, its visible manifestation intensifies. Departure is preceded by a final overwhelming display.
5: qol kanfei hakeruvim nishma ad-hechatzer hachitzonah ('the sound of the wings of the cherubim was heard to the outer court'): the sound is compared to kol El Shaddai bedabro ('the voice of God Almighty when he speaks'). El Shaddai — the patriarchal divine name (Gen 17:1; 28:3; Ex 6:3) emphasizing divine power and sufficiency — appears here as the register for the thunder-sound of the cherubim's wings. The chariot is audible as divine speech.
6: The man in linen obeys the command to take coals. His movement — from the outer area to standing between the wheels — marks him as able to navigate between the human-accessible outer courts and the divine-presence space between the cherubim. He is a mediating figure: priestly linen, scribal tools, access to the divine inner sanctum, commissioned for judgment on the city.
7: yishlach ish halomed et-yado el-toch ha'esh ('the cherub stretched out his hand into the fire'): the fire that the linen-clad man is to take is given to him by a cherub directly — the divine agent of judgment hands the judicial fire to the priestly agent of judgment. The chain of delegation (YHWH → cherub → linen-man → city) distributes the agency of judgment while maintaining its ultimate divine source.
8: vayera el-hakeruvim tabnit yad adam tachtat kanfeihem ('there appeared on the cherubim the form of a human hand beneath their wings'): a detail repeated from 1:8 — the human hands beneath the wings of the living creatures. In chapter 1 this was part of the initial vision; here the identification reinforces that these are the same figures. The human element in the divine chariot emphasizes the relational/personal nature of the divine presence.
9: ofan echad etzel hakerub echad ('one wheel beside each cherub'): the four-wheel structure of the chariot repeated from 1:15-21. kemar'eh even tarshish ('like the appearance of tarshish stone'): tarshish stone is variously identified as beryl, chrysolite, or topaz — a lustrous greenish-blue gem. The same description in 1:16; Daniel 10:6; and Rev 21:20 places tarshish consistently in the register of divine-appearance glory.
10: The four wheels are identical to each other (mareh echad laarba'atam) — no hierarchy, no differentiation among the wheels. This uniformity emphasizes the perfect integration and unified movement of the chariot. The divine throne-chariot has no structural hierarchy among its wheels, just as the four living creatures were coordinate rather than subordinate to each other.
11: el-ha-rosh yelu acharav yelu ('toward the head they go; after the head they go'): direction of movement determined by the head — wherever the rosh turns, the entire chariot turns without rotating. This describes perfect responsiveness to a single will: the chariot is omnidirectional precisely because it is perfectly subordinate to the divine will directing it.
12: kol-besaram vegabotam veyedeihem vekanfeihem vehaofanim mele'im einayim saviv ('their entire bodies, their backs, their hands, their wings, and the wheels — all full of eyes around'): the omni-perceptive nature of the divine chariot — eyes on every surface. The divine presence is omniscient as well as omnipresent. Nothing in the created order is outside YHWH's perception; the chariot's many eyes embody the truth that 'his eyes are on all his ways' (Jer 16:17; Prov 15:3).
13: lagalgal qora lahem ('to the wheel it was called 'the whirling thing''): hagalgal — possibly an intensified form of ofan (wheel) emphasizing rapid rotation. Ezekiel notes what the creatures themselves call their wheels — an insider detail that gives the vision the quality of overheard divine speech, not merely observed phenomenon.
14: arba panim le'echad ('four faces belonging to one'): the four faces of each creature listed — but here, significantly, the first face is hakerub (the cherub face) rather than the shor (ox) of 1:10. The substitution has generated extensive scholarly discussion: possibly 'cherub face' = the ox face seen from the cherubic perspective, or possibly a deliberate difference marking the Jerusalem temple context. Either way, the identification of the creatures as cherubim is being established.
15: vatinase hakeruvim ('the cherubim rose up'): the first movement of the chariot-cherubim since the vision of departure began. They are identified: hi hachayyah asher ra'iti binehar-kevar ('it is the living creature that I saw by the River Chebar'). Ezekiel explicitly connects the two visions — the creatures of chapter 1 are the same as those in the temple vision. The continuity of the divine chariot in both locations affirms that the same divine presence was at the river and in the temple.
16: beseet hakeruvim et-kanfeihem larus me'al ha'aretz lo-yisabbu haofanim miletselam ('when the cherubim went, the wheels went beside them; when the cherubim lifted their wings to rise from the earth, the wheels did not turn from beside them'): perfect synchronization — the wheels do not act independently of the cherubim. The whole chariot moves as a unity, every part responsive to the same animating spirit.
17: ki ruach hachayah bahem ('for the spirit of the living creature was in them'): the ruach (spirit/breath/wind) of the living creatures animates the wheels — they share a single animating principle. In 1:20-21, this same ruach was the key to the chariot's movement. The divine chariot is not a mechanism but a living, spiritually unified entity — which is why it moves in perfect responsiveness to the divine will.
18: vayetse kevod YHWH me'al miftan habayit ('the glory of YHWH departed from over the threshold of the house'): the critical moment — the glory leaves the temple building. From v4 (glory at the threshold) to v18 (glory departing from the threshold), the stage is set: the divine presence has now moved outside the building entirely. This is not merely the ark being captured (1 Sam 4) — it is the voluntary withdrawal of YHWH's presence from his own house in response to its defilement.
19: vaya'amdu petach sha'ar beit YHWH hamizrachit ('they stood at the entrance of the east gate of the house of YHWH'): the glory moves to the east gate — the gate through which the sun rises and toward which the apostate priests had been bowing (8:16). The deliberate choice of the east gate for the glory's penultimate resting place may be a reversal: the gate that was used for solar worship now receives the true divine glory as it exits. Compare 43:1-4 where the glory returns through this same east gate.
20: hi hachayyah asher ra'iti tachat Elohei Yisrael binehar-kevar ('it is the living creature that I saw under the God of Israel by the River Chebar'): the explicit identification, stated plainly and definitively. The creatures beneath the divine throne at the Chebar River and the cherubim in the Jerusalem temple are the same. This retrospective identification validates both visions simultaneously and affirms that Israel's God is not geographically limited — he was on a moveable throne at the Chebar River, and that same throne now departs from Jerusalem.
21: arba panim le'echad ve'arba knafayim le'echad ('four faces for each one and four wings for each one'): Ezekiel catalogs the identifying features of the cherubim to clinch the identification with the chapter 1 creatures. The enumeration is precise: four faces, four wings (vs. Isaiah's six-winged seraphim in Isa 6), with human hands beneath the wings. The consistent anatomy across the two visions is the proof of identity.
22: udemut peneihem hemah hapanim asher ra'iti al-nehar-kevar ('the likeness of their faces — they were the faces that I saw at the River Chebar'): the chapter ends with the full identification confirmed. The vision has now come full circle from chapter 1: the living creatures of the throne-chariot are the cherubim of the temple; the same divine kavod was present in Babylon and in Jerusalem; and now it is departing from Jerusalem. The next move of the glory (11:23 — to the Mount of Olives) completes the departure sequence.
